extracted_information: The provided content comprehensively details seccomp (secure computing mode) system call filtering within the context of Kubernetes, including its integration with container runtimes like Docker, security configurations, and best practices. Seccomp, a Linux kernel feature since version 2.6.12, restricts the system calls a process can make, thereby sandboxing its privileges. Kubernetes allows automatically applying seccomp profiles to Pods and containers.

example_seccomp_profiles.name: audit.json
example_seccomp_profiles.description: Logs all syscalls of the process without restricting them.

example_seccomp_profiles.name: violation.json
example_seccomp_profiles.description: Errors on any syscall, demonstrating a highly restrictive (and usually impractical) profile.

example_seccomp_profiles.name: fine-grained.json
example_seccomp_profiles.description: Allows a specific set of necessary syscalls while denying all others by default.

features.name: Seccomp in Linux
features.description: Sandboxes privileges of a process, restricting system calls from userspace into the kernel.

features.name: Kubernetes Seccomp Integration
features.description: Allows automatic application of seccomp profiles loaded onto a node to Pods and containers.
details: Supports `type: Localhost` for custom profiles (e.g., `localhostProfile: profiles/audit.json`).
Supports `type: RuntimeDefault` to use the container runtime's default profile.
Can be configured as the default for all workloads via kubelet `--seccomp-default` flag (stable since v1.27).

features.name: Container Runtime Default Profiles (RuntimeDefault)
features.description: Most container runtimes (Docker, containerd, CRI-O) provide a sane set of default allowed/disallowed syscalls.
features.details: Aims to provide strong security defaults while preserving workload functionality. Can differ between runtimes and versions.

features.name: Local Kubernetes Cluster Setup (kind)
features.description: Facilitates creating single-node Kubernetes clusters in Docker, allowing seccomp profiles to be mounted into node filesystems (`/var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/profiles`).

features.name: Syscall Auditing
features.description: Using `SCMP_ACT_LOG` allows logging all syscalls, helping identify those required by a process (e.g., `http-echo` process syscalls appear in `/var/log/syslog`).

features.name: Syscall Restriction
features.description: Using `SCMP_ACT_ERRNO` as default action denies unspecified syscalls, enforcing strict security.

seccomp_profile_actions.SCMP_ACT_LOG: Logs syscalls, useful for auditing and profile creation.
seccomp_profile_actions.SCMP_ACT_ERRNO: Returns an error (EPERM) for disallowed syscalls.

security_configurations.container_runtime_default_profiles: Defined by the specific container runtime (e.g., Docker, containerd, CRI-O).
security_configurations.unconfined_mode: Seccomp disabled; processes can make any syscall.

best_practices: Identify the specific syscall privileges required for workloads.
Craft fine-grained seccomp profiles that grant only necessary privileges to container processes.
Utilize `RuntimeDefault` seccomp profiles as a sane and secure default where applicable.
For workloads that fail with `RuntimeDefault`, consider: running as `Unconfined`, disabling `SeccompDefault` on specific nodes, or creating a custom seccomp profile.
When introducing `SeccompDefault` in production, enable it on a subset of nodes and test thoroughly before a cluster-wide rollout.
Use `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` in `securityContext` for containers to enhance security.
bpf_profiles: The document primarily discusses 'seccomp profiles' which are implemented under the hood using Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF) programs. It does not provide explicit details on BPF profiles as separate entities but describes the JSON structure for seccomp profiles, which translate to BPF filters.
